ICIMOD's research programme on the koshi-basin, covering downstream impacts, agricultural dependency, and flood dynamics. Supports the geopolitical significance of Nepal's eastern river system.
ICIMOD's research programme on the koshi-basin, covering downstream impacts, agricultural dependency, and flood dynamics. Supports the geopolitical significance of Nepal's eastern river system.

Koshi basin supports >40 million people Contributes disproportionately high sediment loads to the Ganges system Koshi irrigates ~500,000–600,000 ha in Bihar The Koshi is known as the "River of Sorrow" in Bihar due to shifting course and catastrophic flooding
Central to hydro-geopolitics and ganges-contribution. Also relevant to sediment-as-design-constraint given extreme sediment loads. .
Detailed assessment of sediment dynamics, erosion, and environmental threats in the koshi-basin. Provides the evidence base for treating sediment as a central engineering and planning constraint.

Secondary legal analysis by Sushil Parajuli of the standard NEA hydropower Power Purchase Agreement text, covering Availability Declaration penalties, reserve margin, curtailment risk, and force majeure constraints.

chunks.h: NEA Standard Hydropower PPA Clauses — AD Penalty, Reserve Margin, and Curtailment
chunks.s: Secondary legal analysis by Sushil Parajuli of the standard NEA hydropower Power Purchase Agreement text, covering Availability Declaration penalties, reserve margin, curtailment risk, and force majeure constraints.

Compensation = [0.80 × min(Monthly Contract Energy, Availability Declaration) − (Actual Delivered + Forced Outage + Scheduled Outage + Force Majeure Energy)] × Purchase Rate for that Month. If result is negative, payment is zero. Establishes that if monthly en
In the wet season (peak months Ashadh–Kartik), NEA/LDC can withhold 10% of Contract Energy or Availability Declaration as a "Reserve Margin" with ZERO compensation to the IPP.

No clause in Article 10 exempts an IPP from AD penalties when the generation shortfall is caused by NEA/NLDC dispatch curtailment. The penalty formula applies irrespective of cause (aside from Force Majeure).
30-year take-or-pay: NEA is obligated to pay for 100% of Contracted Energy from COD. Schedule 3 provides up to 72 cumulative hours (3 days) of unpaid outage per year.

Narrow definition. Covers only extraordinary events (e.g., floods exceeding design flood discharge from Schedule 1). Routine grid/transmission/substation failures are NOT force majeure. IPPs cannot invoke FM relief for standard grid failures.

No statutory basis found. Only the Article 10.1(Ka) 10% reserve margin exists in the PPA text. The 16% figure appears to be an industry misinterpretation — likely 10% reserve margin plus an erroneous 6% from an unknown source. No NEA, ERC, or PPA document ment

ERC Notice (Rule 2076/2019, Schedule 5) requires hydropower sellers to submit monthly Availability Declarations 30 days before the month starts. The ERC Electricity Regulatory Commission Act (2017) gives the Commission authority over PPAs. Under SVLCLaw (2022)

chunks.h: Sahas Urja (Solu Khola, 86 MW) — The Benchmark
chunks.s: Final build cost: NPR 172–175 million/MW (industry average often exceeds NPR 200–250M) FY 2080/81 revenue: NPR 2.648 billion Net profit: NPR 902.33 million (114.66% YoY growth) Gross margin: 95.54%; Net margin: 23.61% Plant Load Factor: 69.05%, achieving 87–89

Chilime's "Regular/Excess" billing architecture produces an effective blended rate of NPR 7.57/kWh vs. a typical private IPP's NPR 5.52/kWh — a 37.1% revenue advantage. Chilime classified 85% of its 156 GWh generation as "Regular" at NPR 8.17/kWh.

Sanima Mai Hydropower paid NPR 13.77 million in non-life premiums against NPR 764 million gross revenue = 1.80% of top-line. Within an ~15% OPEX envelope, insurance consumes over 10% of operating budget.

Sahas Urja Limited is the critical positive benchmark in Nepal's private IPP landscape. It demonstrates that profitability in a fixed-tariff environment is dictated by capital efficiency and operational optimization, not anomalous PPA rates.

Entity: Private IPP, incorporated February 2014, converted to public September 2014 Asset: 86 MW Solu Khola (Dudh Koshi) Hydroelectric Project, Run-of-River Location: Solukhumbu District, Koshi Province Q-design: Q41 (~41% probability of exceedance) PPA: Signe

Metric | Value | ---|---| Build cost (final) | NPR 172–175 million/MW | Total project cost | NPR 14.7 billion | Electricity sales revenue | NPR 2.648 billion | Net profit | NPR 902.33 million (+114.66% YoY) | Gross profit margin | 95.54% | Net profit margin |

1. Capital efficiency: ~NPR 173M/MW vs. industry average often exceeding NPR 200–250M/MW 2. Escalation retention: Achieved COD on time → all 8 annual 3% escalations intact 3. Scale: 86 MW means 19.28% dry energy mix still generates massive absolute NPR 8.40/kW

Chairman: Him Prasad Pathak Also controls: 51% stake in Times Energy (341 MW Budhi Gandaki PRoR, NPR 52.5B syndication) IPPAN: Secretary, Executive Committee — direct lobbying access to MoEWRI, ERC, NEA Prior projects: Upper Hugdi Khola (5 MW)

The "Triple-Authority Conflict" — NEA simultaneously functioning as sole off-taker (buyer), system dispatcher (NLDC), and autonomous penalty administrator — distorts IPOs, rights share issuances, and DSCR metrics. Retail investors on NEPSE are downstream victi

chunks.s: Company | MW | IPO Year | IPO Amount (NPR Cr) | Rights Issue | Rights Amount (NPR Cr) | Rights Use | ---|---|---|---|---|---|---| Chilime Hydropower | 22.1 | 2010 | 24.0 | None | — | Holding company expansion (Sanjen, Rasuwagadhi) | Upper Tamakoshi | 456.0 | 2

Chilime operated profitably for 7 years before its 2010 IPO — distributing 35% cash dividends within a year. Modern IPOs are floated pre-commissioning, dumping execution risk onto retail investors. Today's investors buy debt-repayment vehicles, not dividend-yi

IPO prospectuses detail PPA tariff rates to project revenues, but fail to model scenarios where up to 16% of projected revenue is clawed back by the off-taker through structural AD (Availability Declaration) penalties.
Standard model: promoter equity → bank debt → COD → operational cash flow → IPO as exit. Nepal model: pre-COD IPO → rights shares within 24–36 months → proceeds used to prevent bank foreclosure.
Hydropower equities grew from ~6% to ~16% of NEPSE total market capitalization. During FY 2020/21 liquidity surge: NPR 102B → NPR 342B.

"Promoters injected 2–6 Crore NPR/MW out-of-pocket to cover loan repayments" — based on regulatory corpus, not verified primary data "103 MW of small projects required NPR 2.5 billion of additional equity to stop foreclosure" — parameters align with global rep

The wiki argues that AEPC, not NEA, is the natural home for three emerging solar programmes:

agrivoltaics-and-land requires lease-template law, revenue-sharing contracts with smallholder cooperatives, and crop-verification protocols. These are AEPC-format skills, not NEA-format skills. Terai solar at scale likely runs through AEPC's cooperative networ

As NEA grid reaches former off-grid villages (~8.6% of households still grid-absent, nea), each AEPC mini-grid becomes a grid-edge asset. AEPC-NEA integration protocol (who owns what, tariff structure, BESS-sharing) is currently ad-hoc and should be formalized

Ankhu Khola Jalvidhyut Company Limited (AKJCL) is a 42.9 MW run-of-river hydropower project. Beyond its generation capacity, Ankhu Khola is a textbook example of the hydropower rights-share bailout pattern documented in Nepal's capital markets.

Ankhu Khola Jalvidhyut Company Limited (AKJCL) is a 42.9 MW run-of-river hydropower project. Beyond its generation capacity, Ankhu Khola is a textbook example of the hydropower rights-share bailout pattern documented in Nepal's capital markets.

Ankhu Khola's capital markets history exemplifies the structural dynamic where Nepal's retail equity investors absorb commercial bank debt: 2013: IPO raised NPR 20 crore for construction funding 2024: Rights issue of NPR 120 crore at a 1:1.5 ratio — a 150% dil

Arun-3 is designed as a peaking RoR plant — 3.4 hours of full rated output per day, delivered through an underground powerhouse with four Francis turbines. This means its 900 MW nameplate translates into a much smaller day-long energy contribution than its cap

Design energy of ~3,971 GWh/yr leaves Nepal with only 21.9% (~870 GWh) as royalty-free share. The remaining output goes to the Indian grid — making Arun-3 an export-first asset with a domestic free-share component. How this interacts with dry-season firm-power

At ~USD 1.6 billion total cost, Arun-3 is a 30-year BOOT concession — built, owned, operated, and eventually transferred by India's state utility SJVN. The economics depend heavily on whether the 400 kV cross-border evacuation line to Sitamarhi (only 58% compl

SJVN, an Indian state utility, develops, owns, and operates the project through its subsidiary SAPDC. This is the reference case for India-developed export-first hydropower in Nepal — a structure that the domestic-led-hydro-strategy thesis argues against for s

Original commissioning target was March 2025; SJVN has formally applied to extend COD to September 2026. Every year of delay compounds Nepal's storage-deficit exposure because Arun-3 is already counted in NEA's near-term planning. The evacuation line dependenc

NEA currently allocates output from Trishuli (25 MW) and Chilime (22 MW) — older NEA-owned plants — to the Bangladesh export quota. India's CEA has approved these specific 40 MW until October 2, 2029.

Nov 2025 (7th JSC meeting): Nepal and Bangladesh agreed to add 20 MW (total 60 MW) from June 2026 Bangladesh's long-term ambition: import 9,000 MW from Nepal by 2040 Alternative routes under study: Barapukuria, Panchagarh/Thakurgaon, Bheramara, Comilla corrido

1. Buyer diversification. Reduces Nepal's monopsony dependence on India's IEX for export revenue 2. USD pricing. Bangladesh trade in dollars hedges against INR-NPR forex risk — significant given nea's NPR 2.5B forex losses in FY 2024/25 3. India as gatekeeper.

chunks.h: 1. NEA can build solar on-schedule, on-budget, on-spec.
chunks.s: Bidur/Trishuli was delivered within the planned window and within the WB-project cost envelope. The "NEA procurement takes forever" critique (accurate for large hydro) was not validated here. Utility solar is fundamentally easier to deliver than hydropower; le

A 263 m double-curvature arch dam — Nepal's largest planned dam structure — with a reservoir volume of ~3.3 km³. This is a different engineering class from every operating plant in Nepal's fleet. The reservoir area (~63 km²) and displacement footprint (8,117 h

Budhigandaki's defining economic feature is its seasonal split: 42% of annual output comes in winter (1.41 billion units of a total 3.38 billion). For comparison, the average RoR plant delivers 15–25% of its energy in the dry season. This makes Budhigandaki th

Structured as 70% debt / 30% equity, with the government holding 80% and nea 20%. The winter tariff (NPR 12.40/kWh) is nearly double the monsoon rate (NPR 7.10/kWh) — a pricing differential that reflects storage's value premium: shifting water from monsoon sur

After a politically blocked Chinese Gezhouba Group proposal, the project is now structured as a domestic investment. Government ownership (80%) plus NEA (20%) through Budhigandaki Company Limited. The proposed 50-year license term is longer than the standard 3

After years of repeated modality formation, the investment framework was approved by Cabinet in January 2026. Bids for permanent camps and an access bridge were invited in April 2026 — the first concrete procurement. Construction is targeted from January 2028

A peaking run-of-river design with 6-hour regulating pondage. The 103.7 m gross head and 36 m³/s design discharge define its hydraulic envelope. But Chameliya's engineering significance is not in its design parameters — it is in the catastrophic failure of its

Annual design generation of 184.2 GWh. Actual generation for FY2080/81: 133,968 MWh. The gap is not a hydrological design issue — it is an operational reality of a plant built at extreme cost, commissioned a decade late. Parameter | Value | -----------|-------

Chameliya is the wiki's primary case study in state capital inefficiency. Built at USD 226 million for 30 MW — $7.5 million/MW, roughly 3–5× the global benchmark. For context, sahas-urja (86 MW, private IPP) was built at ~NPR 172–175 million/MW (~$1.3–1.4 mill

Eleven years from civil works start (2007) to COD (2018), against an original target of June 2011. This is the definitive cautionary example for buildability. Compare with upper-tamakoshi (~7 years) and tanahu-hydropower (still incomplete after ~10 years). Par

Chameliya represents one of the most troubled infrastructure developments in Nepal's modern history: Civil works began: 2007–2008 (China Gezhouba Group Corporation — civil; Korea Hydro & Nuclear Power — electro-mechanical) Original COD target: June 2011 Initia

chunks.h: Internal Generation Cost vs Commercial Reality
chunks.s: Chameliya has no commercial Power Purchase Agreement. As a directly NEA-owned asset, its energy is accounted for via internal transfer pricing. For FY2080/81: Metric | Value | ---|---| Total generation | 133,968 MWh | Operation cost | NPR 58.32M (NPR 0.44/kWh)

chunks.h: Why Chameliya Matters to This Wiki
chunks.s: Chameliya is the structural mirror-image of ppa-pricing: a state utility that tolerates $7.5M/MW capital costs on its own projects — and records the resulting energy at NPR 3.04/kWh — simultaneously enforces NPR 4.80/8.40 PPA caps on private IPPs that must ful

Dudhkoshi's design pairs a 200–220 m dam with an underground main powerhouse (600 MW) and a dam-toe ecological flow powerhouse (70 MW). The 200 MW pumped-storage component — Nepal's first "water battery" — turns the reservoir into a grid-balancing asset. But a

The reservoir (~1,581 MCM) and 200 MW pumped-storage addition make Dudhkoshi the single largest dry-season lever in Nepal's planned pipeline. Compare with the current operating storage fleet: kulekhani-cascade at 106 MW combined, and tanahu-hydropower at 140 M

Design energy of ~3,377 GWh/yr from a storage plant — unlike RoR, this energy can be concentrated in dry-season months and peak hours, maximizing revenue from higher winter tariffs and evening peaking prices. Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Annual des

AIIB sovereign financing is proposed but not yet committed — the typical latency between "proposed" and "approved" for projects at this scale is several years. Nepal will need to demonstrate fiscal space, guarantee structures, and procurement readiness to move

Four decades of feasibility work (ADB-supported since the 1980s) with land acquisition restarting only in 2021. The current timeline targets construction from ~2027 and COD ~2031. This schedule carries a credibility tax that Nepali storage projects make full:

Dudhkoshi matters on three axes at once, and it is the combination that makes it structurally important: 1. Scale. 635–670 MW of reservoir storage in a system where the entire current storage fleet is 106 MW (kulekhani-cascade) and the only storage actually un

The Gandaki (Narayani) basin is Nepal's central major river system, draining ~32,148 km² within Nepal. Its tributaries include Kali Gandaki, Marsyangdi, Budhi Gandaki, Trishuli, Seti, Madi, and East Rapti. The Kali Gandaki gorge between Annapurna and Dhaulagiri drops >5,500 verti

The Gandaki (Narayani) basin is Nepal's central major river system, draining ~32,148 km² within Nepal. Its tributaries include Kali Gandaki, Marsyangdi, Budhi Gandaki, Trishuli, Seti, Madi, and East Rapti. The Kali Gandaki gorge between Annapurna and Dhaulagir

Fact | Value | ------|-------| Area in Nepal | 32,148 km² | Average discharge (border) | 1,952 m³/s (~61.6 BCM/yr) | Monsoon share | 74–80% | Peak-to-dry ratio | 22–30:1 | Gross hydropower potential | 19,803 MW (27.3% of national total) |

The most mature "buildable" basin. Hosts Nepal's early landmark projects — Kali Gandaki A (144 MW), Middle Marsyangdi (70 MW), and the kulekhani-cascade (on a tributary). The corridor benefited from road access (Prithvi and Siddhartha highways) and historical

sediment-as-design-constraint — the Kali Gandaki A plant is the definitive case study for turbine erosion. Suspended sediment spikes from 20 ppm (dry) to 50,000 ppm (monsoon). 65 million tonnes/year pass the dam site. More developed does not mean fully tapped.

The Gandak system irrigates ~1.5–2 million ha in Bihar and eastern Uttar Pradesh. The Gandak Treaty (1959) gave Nepal only 15 MW from a major barrage — one of the most cited examples of treaty asymmetry. See hydro-geopolitics.

The connected network keeps Kabeli as a branch system: a southern trunk up to Soyak, one arm toward Godak, and one arm toward Phidim/Amarpur. The branch ends are not artificially connected to one another. This matters for reader trust. A single forced line wou

Public decision: default-visible, caveated. The branch topology is source-supported and has no remaining endpoint gaps. The caveat is route precision: the current manual trace is still a document-grounded reconstruction, not a digitized alignment sheet.

The highest-value improvement is to digitize the branch geometry more carefully from the Kabeli IEE route figures while preserving the trunk, Godak branch, and Amarpur/Phidim branch as separate network features.

Three things that make Karnali Solar the institutional template rather than just another remote project:

chunks.h: 1. It is the first formal NEA–AEPC co-delivery structure at scale.
chunks.s: Historically AEPC delivered off-grid solar through community cooperatives; NEA delivered grid-tied solar through tenders. Karnali Solar Energy Project is the first programme where NEA provides O&M support and technical backstop to AEPC-delivered off-grid syste

chunks.h: 2. It validates solar + BESS economics at the remote site
chunks.s: Diesel generation in remote Karnali sites costs NPR 60–90/kWh (delivered fuel + logistics + wear). Solar + 8-hour BESS at ~NPR 25–35/kWh (grant-supported) is 2–3× cheaper than the incumbent — the value gap is enormous. This is the highest-leverage per-kWh sola

chunks.h: 3. It prepares the grid-edge integration question
chunks.s: As NEA's grid finally reaches former off-grid villages (~8.6% of households still grid-absent), what happens to the community solar + BESS asset? Karnali Solar Energy Project pre-formalises this: the asset continues to operate, is tariff-integrated with the NE

The Khimti Khola in Dolakha district is one of Nepal's earlier IPP-developed hydropower corridors. The cascade includes multiple projects at different stages, with the original Khimti-I plant serving as one of the country's first large private hydropower investments.

Khimti-I (commissioned 2000) was one of Nepal's first major private hydropower projects and helped establish the BOOT (Build-Own-Operate-Transfer) model that subsequent IPPs followed. The Khimti corridor became a test case for whether private developers could

The khimti-dhalkebar-corridor (220 kV) was purpose-built to evacuate power from the Khimti Khola plants into the national grid backbone, connecting the Dolakha valley to the Dhalkebar substation on the east-west corridor.

The Khimti Khola is a tributary of the Tamakoshi River in the koshi-basin. The broader Khimti-Tamakoshi corridor in Dolakha includes multiple significant projects including upper-tamakoshi (456 MW), tamakoshi-v (99.8 MW), and tamakoshi-3 (287 MW).

Khungri matters less as a proven hydro-hybrid project and more as a test of whether NEA's national tender can reach non-Terai grid nodes at portfolio scale:

chunks.h: 1. It is one of the largest mid-hill awards in the LoI table.
chunks.s: At 50 MW, Khungri is at the high end of the 960 MW tender's project-size distribution. Its award price of NPR 5.21/kWh is still inside the low end of the national tariff band, which makes it a useful check on whether mid-hill grid-adjacent solar can compete wi

chunks.h: 2. It should be treated as an IPP LoI until PPA/COD evidence appears.
chunks.s: The corrected NEA source does not establish NEA ownership, COD, or a commissioned hybrid operating configuration. Until later PPA or commissioning records are available, the correct status is planned / LoI-awarded, not operating and not NEA-owned.

chunks.h: 3. It keeps the hybrid-siting question alive, but does not prove it.
chunks.s: Khungri sits in the same broad western mid-hill geography where solar, substations, and hydropower evacuation corridors can overlap. That makes it relevant to hybrid-siting-logic, but the LoI record alone does not prove shared hydro-solar substation operation.

Largest gross potential of any basin in Nepal per wecs-hydropower-potential-2019. Contains the dudhkoshi-storage project (670 MW) and upper-tamakoshi (456 MW, largest operating plant). The Arun corridor hosts Upper Arun (900 MW, under construction).

Extreme sediment loads — ICIMOD framing puts the basin near 120 million tonnes/year into the Ganges system, or nearly 25% of the total sediment load. See sediment-as-design-constraint. Flood hazard — known as the "River of Sorrow" in Bihar due to catastrophic
Koshi irrigates ~500,000–600,000 ha in Bihar. Supports >40 million people downstream. Contributes disproportionate sediment to the Ganges system. Central to hydro-geopolitics and ganges-contribution.

Kulekhani I is a seasonal-storage reservoir: a 114 m rockfill dam creates a headpond that captures monsoon inflow (~June–September) and releases it through the dry season (~November–May) to Kulekhani II and III downstream. This is the only facility in Nepal wh

Kulekhani's seasonal generation pattern is what distinguishes it from RoR. While RoR plants generate ~70–80% of their annual energy in the four monsoon months, Kulekhani shifts a substantial portion into the dry season. The combined 279.8 GWh in FY 2081/82 is

Beyond energy, Kulekhani provides services that RoR plants cannot: Black start capability — KL-I can restart the Kathmandu valley grid without external power, a critical grid-resilience function Peak-hour dispatch — the reservoir can ramp generation to match e

Kulekhani I was commissioned in 1982, making it Nepal's first major storage hydropower project — and, 40+ years later, still its only one. The cascade additions (KL-II in 1986, KL-III in 2019) extended the system but did not add new reservoir capacity. Compare

Kulekhani was built with World Bank financing in the late 1970s as part of Nepal's first generation of utility-scale hydropower. At the time, Nepal had fewer than 100 MW of installed capacity, nearly all run-of-river. The choice to build a storage reservoir 50
ir storage. Kulekhani is not the exception that proves the rule — it is the exception that became the rule, a data point that should have been the first of many and instead became the only one.

chunks.s: In May 2021, on the eve of production testing, a wall of Likhu-4's desanding basin suffered a catastrophic structural collapse. The developer publicly blamed the Indian civil contractor, SSNR Projects, alleging construction defects. A desanding basin failure a

chunks.h: Financial Position — Constrained by Legacy Costs
chunks.s: Green Ventures currently carries a CARE-NP BB rating — two notches below investment grade and below both Likhu-2 (BBB-) and Likhu-1 (BB+). The rating is constrained by: 1. High capital costs — the redesign from 120 MW to 52.4 MW did not fully recapture sunk en

Like the rest of the cascade, Likhu-4 evacuates through New Khimti. Following the late-2023 200 MVA transformer upgrade, Likhu-4 was among the first cascade plants to receive CEA export approval for Dhalkebar–Muzaffarpur wheeling into the Indian day-ahead mark

Madhya Marsyangdi (Middle Marsyangdi) is a 70 MW operating hydropower project on the Marsyangdi River — one of Nepal's established generation assets with a multi-decade operational track record.

Madhya Marsyangdi sits in the middle of one of Nepal's most productive hydropower corridors. The Marsyangdi River, a tributary of the Trishuli/Narayani system in the Gandaki basin, hosts a cascade of projects: Upper Marsyangdi A (50 MW), Madhya Marsyangdi (70

As an operating plant, Madhya Marsyangdi provides a different lens from the pre-COD and under-construction projects that dominate the pipeline: actual generation data, real maintenance costs, and historical PLF performance under the standard PPA framework. Ope

The Marsyangdi valley's generation feeds into the national grid via the Marsyangdi corridor infrastructure, connecting to the east-west backbone. The Upper Marsyangdi 220 kV line provides the key evacuation route for this cascade.

Middle Marsyangdi (MMHPS) is a 70 MW peaking run-of-river plant in Lamjung District, Gandaki Province — the middle anchor of the 170 MW Marsyangdi cascade. Commissioned in December 2008, it diverts flow from the Marsyangdi River originating in glacier-fed Tili

chunks.h: The Marsyangdi Cascade — 170 MW in One Valley
chunks.s: MMHPS sits between Upper Marsyangdi A (50 MW, operating, upstream) and Marsyangdi Besi (50 MW, under construction, downstream) — a cumulative 170 MW in the Marsyangdi valley of the Gandaki basin. This is one of Nepal's most concentrated generation corridors. T

chunks.h: The Contrast That Matters — Design vs Actual
chunks.s: MMHPS's 107.6% of design generation (427.9 GWh against 398 GWh) is the highest performance ratio in the NEA large-plant fleet. Compare: Plant | MW | Design (GWh) | FY 2081/82 (GWh) | % of Design | ---|---|---|---|---| Middle Marsyangdi | 70 | 398 | 427.9 | 107

chunks.h: Renewal Cycle — A Younger Plant with a Different Problem
chunks.s: At 16 years post-commissioning, MMHPS is entering the phase where routine overhauls begin to graduate toward major rehabilitation. The FY 2081/82 work scope — Unit 2 overhaul, desander basin restoration, spillway chute rebuild with M60 concrete and epoxy coati

The Mahakali river forms Nepal's far-western border with India. It is a transboundary basin with far smaller current development than the three major eastern systems. The basin's significance is primarily geopolitical — it is the site of the pancheshwar multipurpose project and t

The Mahakali river forms Nepal's far-western border with India. It is a transboundary basin with far smaller current development than the three major eastern systems. The basin's significance is primarily geopolitical — it is the site of the pancheshwar multip
Fact | Value | ------|-------| Average discharge | ~600 m³/s | Monsoon share | ~73% | Lakes in basin (Nepal side) | 16 |

The Mahakali Treaty (1996) entitles Nepal to 50% of pancheshwar's output (6,480 MW total, or ~3,240 MW for Nepal). After 28+ years, Nepal has received zero megawatts from this treaty. See hydro-geopolitics. The Mahakali already supports irrigation via the Maha

The basin is less about sheer resource volume and more about the diplomatic relationship with India. The failure of Pancheshwar to materialize is one of the strongest examples of how hydro-geopolitics shapes — and stalls — Nepal's energy future.

chunks.h: Why the zone is strategically interesting
chunks.s: Three genuine reasons, despite the grid-distance challenge:

chunks.h: 1. It is the only Nepal zone where winter is the best solar month.
chunks.s: Winter clear-sky days are more numerous than summer clear-sky days here (summer can have valley-mist + high-altitude storms; winter is cloudless). The complementarity argument in solar-hydro-complementarity is strongest here: Zone A in winter generates ~110% o

chunks.h: 2. Off-grid solar + BESS at these sites has extraordinary kWh-value.
chunks.s: Incumbent energy source is mostly: (a) no electricity at all, (b) diesel generation at NPR 60–90/kWh, (c) firewood for heating and cooking. Displacing (b) makes Zone A solar the highest-value per-kWh energy investment in Nepal — 10–15× the utility-tariff-equiv

chunks.h: 3. It is the long-dated solar export corridor.
chunks.s: The ratmate-rasuwagadhi-kerung-400kv line, if completed, opens a trans-Himalayan interconnection to Tibet. Zone A solar, transmitted northward, becomes relevant to China's western-grid imports. This is a 15–20 year story, not a near-term strategy, but Nepal's

Grid distance: Nearest 132 kV substation is typically 100–200 km away. Terrain multiplies per-km transmission cost 3–5×. Local demand: Mustang's total population ~14,000; Dolpa's ~37,000. Grid-scale off-take is absent. Land tenure: Much of upper Mustang and Do

chunks.h: What Zone A is for, realistically
chunks.s: In order of near-term deployability: 1. Off-grid + BESS for remote institutional and community sites — the karnali-solar-energy-project template, extended to Mustang and Dolpa. 2. Diesel-displacement at trekking lodges and research stations — 5–50 kWp each, hi

chunks.h: Why this tender matters (beyond the MW)
chunks.s: Four reasons:

chunks.h: 1. It establishes the price floor for Nepal solar.
chunks.s: The awarded tariff range (4.99–5.54 NPR/kWh) is the first portfolio-scale solar price discovery in Nepal. Earlier rounds (Phase I at 8.40, Phase III around the 6.40 band) were single-digit-count project awards; 960 MW with 63 awards is the first price point th

63 IPPs winning awards implies a functional pipeline of developers, advisors, local EPC firms, and debt financiers. The Nepal solar market was single-digit-developer-deep before this tender; it is now dozens-of-developer-deep. This matters more than the physic

The tender has four binding constraints: land acquisition (Terai agricultural politics), grid integration (penetration levels, forecast management), financial close (local-currency long-tenor debt), PPA discipline (NEA single-buyer liability). If the tender's

chunks.h: 4. It is the test case for agrivoltaics-and-land.
chunks.s: The first awarded projects will surface which land-acquisition model works at scale. If pure-purchase / fee-simple conversion dominates and succeeds, agrivoltaics is merely an add-on. If land-acquisition delays kill > 30% of awards, agrivoltaic lease templates

chunks.h: 1. Land acquisition is the new bottleneck
chunks.s: Phase I–III plants were small enough (0.5–25 MWp) that land was project-specific. Phase IV (960 MW) triggers the land-at-scale question that agrivoltaics-and-land addresses. NEA does not have a land-programme office; this function needs to move to a new body o

The cumulative grid impact study NEA completed under Phase IV anticipates 4–6% instantaneous-penetration events by 2027 when 960 MW is partially commissioned. Forecast management, BESS requirements, and reactive-power compensation need to be designed in now.

Nepal has crossed from chronic shortage into seasonal surplus. It now exports electricity in the monsoon and still imports in the dry season. The country has more electricity than it can sell and less than it needs — at the same time, every year. See seasonal-

The country is far better off than during the classic shortage era, but that recent success can be overstated if the timeline is forgotten: Scheduled load shedding ended in 2018. Before that, outages lasted up to 14 hours/day between 2007 and 2017. World Bank-

Nepal's broader energy system is not electricity-centered. Biomass still dominates. In the FY 2079/80 WECS balance, residential use was 60.75% of final energy, grid electricity 7.23% (up from 4.96% the prior year — a real jump driven by +21.2% electricity cons

Rasuwagadhi is a 111 MW run-of-river hydropower project under construction on the Bhote Koshi River in Rasuwa district — one of the larger projects in Nepal's under-construction pipeline and a key asset in the Chilime Hydropower group portfolio.

Rasuwagadhi is a 111 MW run-of-river hydropower project under construction on the Bhote Koshi River in Rasuwa district — one of the larger projects in Nepal's under-construction pipeline and a key asset in the Chilime Hydropower group portfolio.

Rasuwagadhi Hydropower Company Limited is an NEA subsidiary, structurally nested under Chilime Hydropower Company Limited (CHCL). Chilime holds a 33% stake, while the NEA directly holds 18%, keeping the project within the state umbrella. As a Chilime subsidiar

Rasuwagadhi's PPA was signed in November 2011 under the standardized IPP tariff structure of NPR 4.80/kWh (wet season) and NPR 8.40/kWh (dry season), with a 3% annual escalation cap for eight years. By 2011, regulatory pressure from private IPPs had forced the

Rasuwagadhi has faced delayed Commercial Operation Date (COD) due to disasters, including geological setbacks in the Bhote Koshi corridor. The project trades on its Chilime subsidiary reputation, but its financial trajectory is shaped by the same structural co

Located near Nepal's northern border with Tibet in Rasuwa district, Rasuwagadhi sits in a corridor that includes the Kerung border crossing — an increasingly strategic trade and infrastructure point between Nepal and China. This geographic positioning gives th

chunks.h: The Cascade Model — Paired with Upper Tamakoshi
chunks.s: Tamakoshi V is the first cascade project explicitly designed to operate in tandem with upper-tamakoshi. At 99.8 MW, it captures energy from the tailwater discharge of the 456 MW PROR powerhouse upstream — effectively extracting additional generation from the s

Tamakoshi V is financed by Nepal's Employers Provident Fund (EPF) — the country's largest pension fund. This marks a departure from the commercial bank consortium model that dominates private IPP financing. The EPF model carries distinct structural implication

Tamakoshi Jalvidyut Company Limited (TKJVCL) sits within the same NEA subsidiary architecture that produced upper-tamakoshi (via UKHLL), but with a distinct developer entity and financing source. The relationship: Project | Capacity | Developer | Financing | S

As of the NEA FY 2024/25 annual report, Tamakoshi V has reached 15.34% construction progress. Key milestones: Contractor's camp near headworks and powerhouse area completed Bunker near headworks area completed Batching and crusher plant installed and operation

Tamakoshi V shares the Tama Koshi transmission corridor with the largest concentration of generation capacity in Nepal. The New Khimti substation — originally sized for Upper Tamakoshi (456 MW) — now serves the entire Likhu cascade (213 MW) plus Tamakoshi V (9
Tamor Storage is a proposed 200 MW storage hydropower project on the Tamor River in eastern Nepal, currently at the survey/study stage.
Tamor Storage is a proposed 200 MW storage hydropower project on the Tamor River in eastern Nepal, currently at the survey/study stage.

Storage hydropower is the critical missing piece in Nepal's generation portfolio. Run-of-river projects dominate the fleet — they produce abundant energy during the monsoon but collapse during the dry season when system demand peaks. See storage-deficit and se

Storage projects in Nepal face three compounding challenges: 1. Geology: Dam sites in the young, seismically active Himalaya require extensive investigation 2. Resettlement: Reservoirs displace communities, creating political and social costs that run-of-river

In a system increasingly saturated with monsoon-heavy run-of-river generation, Tamor Storage's value lies not in its annual energy contribution but in its seasonal firmness. A 200 MW reservoir that can deliver into the dry-season evening peak would have outsiz

Trishuli is Nepal's oldest operating hydropower plant, commissioned in 1967 with Indian government funding. Originally 21 MW (7 × 3 MW units), it was rehabilitated in 1995 to 24 MW with six 3.5 MW Francis units and one 3 MW standby unit. As a peaking run-of-ri

l safety upgrade for a plant running 1960s turbine hardware. Powerhouse maintenance covered rotor shaft work on Unit 1, bearing repairs on Units 2, 5, and 6, MIV repair on Unit 2, and oil cooler installation on Unit 1.

chunks.h: The Heritage Plant — 58 Years and Counting
chunks.s: Trishuli was commissioned when Nepal's grid barely existed. At 21 MW, it was the first major power source serving Kathmandu — and for years, it was the grid. Over 58 years, it has accumulated 6,031.9 GWh of lifetime generation, roughly a third of kali-gandaki-

chunks.h: 60% of Design — The Fleeting Rehab Benefit
chunks.s: Trishuli's 60% design-performance ratio is the lowest in the NEA fleet. At 97.7 GWh from a 163 GWh design, the rehab that raised capacity from 21 to 24 MW in 1995 did not prevent progressive output decline. The 17% year-over-year drop (FY 2080/81 to 2081/82) s

The geopolitics preset is not a Nepal-only map. It follows Nepal-linked rivers into the northern Indian plains, Bangladesh, and the Bay of Bengal because the political question is about downstream exposure, not just upstream origin.

chunks.h: How To Read The Geopolitics Map
chunks.s: The geopolitics preset is not a Nepal-only map. It follows Nepal-linked rivers into the northern Indian plains, Bangladesh, and the Bay of Bengal because the political question is about downstream exposure, not just upstream origin.

Read the layers in this order: Map mark | Meaning | ----------|---------| Nepal-linked basin polygons | Where Nepal sits inside the upstream basin geography. | Nepal rivers and downstream rivers | The main water routes from Nepal into the plains. | Ocean route

Do not read the downstream layers as water-control claims. They are exposure claims. The map does not say Nepal controls Bihar, Uttar Pradesh, or Bangladesh water security. It says Nepal-linked rivers pass through sensitive downstream geographies where timing,

“Nepal has 83,000 MW” is a political slogan and a methodological landmine. Hydropower potential categories exist precisely because theoretical river power is not the same as financeable capacity, buildable projects, or firm winter electricity (see firm-power).

“Nepal has 83,000 MW” is a political slogan and a methodological landmine. Hydropower potential categories exist precisely because theoretical river power is not the same as financeable capacity, buildable projects, or firm winter electricity (see firm-power).

Think of a pyramid—quantified in data-potential-pyramid—where each step applies filters the previous step ignores: 1. Theoretical / gross potential — legacy national figures near ~83,000 MW appear in older discourse; WECS (2019) reframed theoretical potential

A large share of gross potential concentrates in three major basins—koshi-basin, karnali-basin, and gandaki-basin—often cited on the order of ~94% of national potential. That concentration matters for cascade externalities, sediment, and geopolitical export co

Potential-category discipline prevents two common failures: inflating creditworthiness with theoretical MW, and confusing annual energy with dry-season dependability. Primary methodology and citations live under wecs-hydropower-potential-2019.

Koshi is the clearest hazard and sediment case in the Nepal-linked downstream story. Its route matters because flood memory, embankment politics, sediment behavior, and dense settlement all meet in North Bihar before the system enters the lower Ganges.
Read it together with north-bihar-kosi-gandak-floodplain. The merge point is the route handoff; the floodplain zone is the human and agricultural exposure context.
This is not a claim that Nepal alone determines Kosi or Ganges outcomes. It is a route and exposure marker for a shared river system.

The NEA Triple-Authority Conflict describes how the Nepal Electricity Authority simultaneously holds three powers over private hydropower producers (IPPs) that, in a well-designed market, would be separated across independent institutions: 1. Dispatcher — thro

When NEA exercises role (1) to curtail a project — citing grid stability, N-1 transmission limits, or low domestic demand — the project's revenue stops. When NEA then exercises role (3), it can apply AD penalties for the generation shortfall that it itself ord

chunks.h: The incentive to favor NEA-owned generation
chunks.s: NEA generates approximately 34% of national power through its own plants and subsidiaries. When NLDC dispatches an NEA-subsidiary plant, NEA pays itself — no cash leaves the institution. When NLDC dispatches a private IPP, NEA must release budget funds. The st

chunks.h: The incentive to favor NEA-owned generation
chunks.s: ists. NLDC's curtailment decisions affecting billions of rupees in annual revenue are made without public criteria. Neither the Auditor General nor any parliamentary committee has formally investigated dispatch discrimination.

The Barahi Hydropower case provides the wiki's primary audited documentation of this double-bind in operation. NEA deducted AD penalties from a sub-10 MW project despite an explicit ERC statutory exemption (ERC Bylaws 2076, Clause 8(2)), and the project was fo

The standard penalty formula documented in investment and tender materials is: Penalty = (0.8 × Contracted Energy − Electricity Output) × Electricity Tariff If a project's generation falls beneath 80% of its contracted energy (or declared availability, dependi

The AD system requires generators to declare their available generation 30 days in advance. For a run-of-river project with no storage reservoir, predicting daily output in a Himalayan monsoon watershed 30 days ahead is not an engineering failure — it is an in

A healthy market would separate the three functions: Independent system operator (ISO) — dispatch decisions separated from NEA's commercial interests. Multiple buyers or open access — IPPs able to sell to alternative off-takers (industrial consumers, export ma

The Triple-Authority Conflict is the institutional mechanism underlying: stranded-generation — curtailment without compensation seasonal-arbitrage-trap — pricing structure that extracts value from IPPs ad-penalties — the penalty mechanics enforced through this

Peak water (in the glacio-hydrological sense used across Hindu Kush-Himalaya work) is the period when a glacierized catchment reaches maximum annual meltwater yield before long-term decline sets in. It is not "peak" in the oil-depletion sense of a single day; it is a multi-decade

Peak water (in the glacio-hydrological sense used across Hindu Kush-Himalaya work) is the period when a glacierized catchment reaches maximum annual meltwater yield before long-term decline sets in. It is not "peak" in the oil-depletion sense of a single day;

chunks.h: Mechanism: more melt before less water
chunks.s: As glaciers retreat, many basins first see higher annual melt — more energy at the surface, longer melt seasons, reconfiguration of flow paths — before entering a regime where ice storage is depleted and late-century flows fall, especially in the low-flow mont

chunks.h: Why it matters for hydropower planning
chunks.s: Nepal's dominant fleet is sensitive to intra-annual and inter-annual hydrographs; see seasonal-mismatch. Peak water defines a planning window: roughly 20-40 years during which glacial contribution is near its maximum for many catchments — not a stable eternity

Retreat does not only alter means; it reconfigures risk. Lake formation, moraine instability, and cascading processes connect peak-water-era construction to glof-risk: the same decades that maximize certain yields also maximize some tail-risk exposures unless

In hydrology, Qx denotes the flow rate (m³/s) that is exceeded X% of the time in the historical record. Q40 is the flow equaled or exceeded 40% of days; Q65 is exceeded 65% of days. Higher-percentile values (Q65, Q70) represent higher-flow points on the flow-d

The seasonal arbitrage trap describes how Nepal can appear to "win" on electricity trade while losing on economic value: the country systematically sells cheap wet-season surplus and buys expensive dry-season power, so headline trade balances mask a destroyed margin on timing.

chunks.s: In Nepal’s major rivers, a large share of annual discharge arrives in the wet season (commonly cited as 75%+ between June and September in basin planning discourse). RoR plants therefore produce a wet-season surplus and a dry-season deficit simultaneously—two

Adding RoR capacity is not neutral: it increases energy when the system is already long, and it does little to change the dry-season floor unless accompanied by storage, diversified winter resources, or imports/exports that are actually available when domestic

9/2010) independently confirmed 20.4 MCM total gross loss (23.9%) and 14 MCM active live storage loss (19.1%). These two independent measurements — physical bathymetry and spatial catchment modeling — converge on the same ~24% gross loss figure. Kulekhani's ~2

Nepal is not failing because it lacks hydropower potential. It is failing to convert hydrological advantage - and now cheap solar complementarity - into firm, deliverable, resilient, financeable, politically executable power. See claim-systems-conversion-failu

The conversion failure is not one bottleneck. It is a stack: 1. seasonal-mismatch — 75%+ of water in four months; hydrology delivers a monsoon pulse, not year-round firm power 2. run-of-river-hydropower — fleet is >90% RoR/PRoR; structurally produces wet-seaso

Agricultural energy use follows the farming geography: 1. Terai belt (Madhesh, Lumbini, Koshi, Sudurpaschim southern districts): ~80% of tubewell irrigation. Shallow and deep tubewells concentrated in the eastern and central Terai where groundwater is accessib

Nepal has an estimated ~100,000+ shallow tubewells (STWs) and several thousand deep tubewells (DTWs) in the Terai, predominantly diesel-powered. The government's policy direction — mentioned in NEA's FY 2024/25 report — is to encourage electricity use for irri

NEA does not report agricultural electricity separately — it falls within the "Others" category (10.48% of FY 2024/25 sales, ~1,183 GWh) which also includes street lighting, temporary connections, and water supply pumping. The actual agriculture-only slice wit

Agriculture in Nepal is where the water-energy-food nexus is most visible: 75% of Nepal's water withdrawals go to agriculture (irrigation), nearly all from surface sources Only ~38% of irrigable land is currently irrigated year-round (WB Water Sector Diagnosti

Agricultural energy demand is likely to grow from a very low base for several reasons: 1. Irrigation expansion — government targets to increase year-round irrigated area from ~38% to >60% by 2035 2. Pump electrification — tubewell diesel-to-electric switching

chunks.h: The five structural facts in this table
1. Fuelwood in household cooking is the single biggest energy flow in the country
chunks.s: 257,226 TJ of fuelwood burned in residential use is 48.3% of all final energy consumed in Nepal. Not industry, not transport. Kitchens. See data-domestic-demand for the companion "<1% of households cook with electricity" fact — together these two numbers are t

chunks.h: 2. Industry is 21%, but it's not factories — it's bricks, cement, and steel rerolling
chunks.s: Inside the 111.3 PJ industrial sector: Coal 33.6 PJ (30.2%) — this is ~99% of all coal consumed in Nepal, almost entirely brick kilns (fixed-chimney Bull's-trench type, ~1,500+ operating), cement clinker, and iron/steel rerolling. Biomass 48.2 PJ (43.3%) — fue

chunks.h: 3. Transport is 10.4%, and it is essentially 100% imported fossil
chunks.s: 55,528 TJ petroleum vs 25 TJ electricity in transport. Split inside petroleum: Diesel 54.9% (trucks, buses, tractors) Petrol 34.0% (motorcycles, cars) ATF 11.1% (aviation — all of Nepal's aviation fuel, 6,191 TJ) Electricity 0.05% (trolley bus + statistical no

Explorer label: Wet/dry season flow Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 8
Explorer label: Wet/dry season flow Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 8 Basin annotations focused on monsoon concentration, winter weakness, and dry-season implications.

This layer makes seasonality explicit on the map. Nepal's hydro advantage is physically real, but its value depends heavily on when water arrives. The annotations help separate annual volume from winter firmness.

Use it with storage shortlist, operating hydropower, and downstream markers. The key question is how much of the basin's energy or flow is wet-season heavy and what that means for dry-season deficits.
Seasonality annotations summarize complex hydrology. They should be read as planning context, not a replacement for plant-level generation modeling or climate-adjusted hydrological studies.

Explorer label: Carto Positron Layer group: Basemaps Feature count: basemap
Explorer label: Carto Positron Layer group: Basemaps Feature count: basemap Neutral light basemap for reading dense hydrology, project, and transmission overlays without heavy terrain shading.

Carto Positron is the default visual ground plane for the explorer. It is intentionally quiet: roads, settlements, borders, water bodies, and labels remain visible, but the style avoids strong terrain color and imagery texture that would compete with project m

Use this basemap when comparing several overlays at once. It is best for dense views such as operating plus construction hydropower, basin polygons with tributaries, or transmission corridors with node layers. If the question is about layer relationships rathe

Because the basemap is visually restrained, it is not the right layer for judging slopes, ridges, road access, reservoir surfaces, or land-cover constraints. Switch to Topographic or Satellite when geography itself is the evidence.

Explorer label: Cross-border lines Layer group: Transmission and trade layers Feature count: 10

Explorer label: Cross-border lines Layer group: Transmission and trade layers Feature count: 10 Conservative line/stub geometries for operational, under-construction, implementation-stage, and planned cross-border links.

This layer draws cross-border links as conservative endpoint connectors or gateway stubs rather than only points. It makes visible the fact that trade is a physical network problem before it is a market story.
Use it to compare operational, under-construction, implementation, and planned links. It helps explain why eastern, central, and western Nepal have different export and import possibilities.
Planned or implementation-stage lines are not bankable capacity until completed and operational. Gateway stubs are intentionally conservative and do not claim the full India-side route.

Explorer label: International gateways Layer group: Transmission and trade layers Feature count: 10
Explorer label: International gateways Layer group: Transmission and trade layers Feature count: 10 Nepal-side gateway points for cross-border interconnections with status and timeline context.

This layer maps the Nepal-side gateway points between Nepal's grid and India's grid. It is central to export strategy, import reliability, and the politics of monetizing hydropower beyond Nepal's domestic demand.

Use it with trade, transmission, and hydropower layers. Interconnections explain why generation capacity alone does not guarantee export revenue: the system needs cross-border capacity, domestic corridors feeding those nodes, and political clearance.
An interconnection marker does not imply unrestricted commercial access. Trade rules, line capacity, synchronization, scheduling, and approvals still determine what can flow.

Explorer label: Downstream dependency zones Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 5 Strategic downstream exposure zones for agriculture, population, floods, dry-season flow, sediment, and delta sensitivity.

This layer turns the route-to-ocean trace into a downstream stakes map. It marks broad regions where Nepal-linked river routes pass through dense agricultural plains, flood-sensitive corridors, and the Bangladesh delta system. For the non-technical reading seq

Use these polygons as strategic context around the river route, not as precise service areas. Important fields: Field | How To Use It | -------|---------------| relatedoriginroutes | Links each zone to the Nepal-origin route traces that pass into or through it

This is not a hydrodynamic model, irrigation command map, population raster, crop-intensity map, or flood hazard model. It does not claim that Nepal controls total water availability in these zones. The correct reading is: these are downstream exposure zones t

Explorer label: Flow measurements Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 4
Explorer label: Flow measurements Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 4 Point markers carrying discharge, annual runoff, monsoon share, and impact notes for major systems.

These markers summarize basin-scale hydrology at strategic downstream reference points. They make the core numbers easier to inspect than a polygon alone: discharge, runoff, monsoon share, and short interpretive notes.

Use the markers to compare basins by flow scale and seasonality. They work best with basin polygons and downstream-system lines, because the marker gives a data point while the lines and polygons show spatial extent.
A single marker cannot represent all seasonal, annual, or sub-basin variation. Treat values as context for planning arguments, not as a substitute for station-level hydrological analysis.

This layer adds named places to the downstream river story so the route and dependency zones are easier to orient. The anchors include major cities, delta nodes, and river-port style points along the Ganges, Kosi/Gandak/Ghaghara plains, Padma, and lower delta.

Use the points to locate the human geography around the river system: Field | How To Use It | -------|---------------| nearbyriversystem | The river system that gives the anchor its map relevance. | relatedoriginroutes | Nepal-linked routes associated with the

These anchors do not imply that a city is supplied only by Nepal-origin rivers. They are context markers for downstream geography, population concentration, floodplain exposure, and route legibility. Coordinates are city/context anchors, not water-intake point

Explorer label: Floating solar Layer group: Solar layers Feature count: 5 Reservoir or future-reservoir floating PV candidates with confidence and capacity-band fields.

This layer shows reservoirs and future-reservoir contexts where floating PV could become a leverage play. The idea is not that floating PV replaces hydro, but that it can use water surfaces and grid-adjacent hydro infrastructure to add dry-season or daytime va
Use it with storage shortlist and hybrid siting logic. A candidate is most interesting when it overlaps with storage, transmission access, and a credible reservoir surface.

Floating PV candidates are early-stage and confidence-tagged. Water-level variation, reservoir operations, environmental constraints, anchoring, grid connection, and institutional ownership all matter.

Explorer label: Power hubs & substations Layer group: Transmission and trade layers Feature count: 39 Named grid hubs and substation/place anchors used to orient the network.
This public point layer gives the reader named grid geography: major substations, hubs, and anchor places used to interpret the transmission network.
Use this layer with the major transmission network and cross-border links. It helps orient the reader and gives corridors recognizable endpoints.
Not every anchor is a surveyed substation coordinate. Some are towns, hubs, or geocoding references, so use the popup basis when precision matters.

Explorer label: Hydro — building Layer group: Hydropower layers Feature count: 180

Explorer label: Hydro — building Layer group: Hydropower layers Feature count: 180 Generation/construction-license projects, useful for seeing near-term capacity pressure on basins and transmission corridors.
This layer maps projects licensed to build or generate, which makes it a pipeline layer rather than a current-supply layer. It is useful for seeing where future MW claims cluster geographically.

Use it beside transmission corridors and basin seasonality. Dense construction clusters can indicate where evacuation pressure, financing risk, and winter-output limitations will matter before the projects become useful system capacity.

A generation license does not guarantee timely commissioning, financing closure, transmission availability, or winter firm energy. Treat this layer as near-term pressure and option value, not delivered electricity.

Explorer label: Hydro — operating Layer group: Hydropower layers Feature count: 81

Explorer label: Hydro — operating Layer group: Hydropower layers Feature count: 81 Operating hydropower projects from the project registry. Marker area scales by capacity, and wiki counts show page coverage.
This layer shows projects that are in operation in the display registry. It is the closest map layer to Nepal's current hydropower asset base, separating built supply from future promises.

Use it to understand the geography of existing generation. Marker size scales with capacity, so large stations stand out visually, but the map should be read together with technology type, river basin, seasonality, and transmission access.

Operating MW is not the same as firm winter power or delivered value. Run-of-river output can be highly seasonal, and a project can be technically operating while still facing evacuation or hydrology constraints.

Explorer label: Hydro — planned Layer group: Hydropower layers Feature count: 311
Explorer label: Hydro — planned Layer group: Hydropower layers Feature count: 311 Survey-stage hydropower licenses: early pipeline signals, not built capacity.
This layer maps projects still at survey or study stage. It is useful for understanding where developers see resource potential, but it sits far upstream from actual generation.
Use it for option density, basin crowding, and long-run project geography. It is especially helpful when comparing potential-heavy regions with the actual operating fleet.

Survey-stage projects should not be counted as supply. Many will change size, ownership, design, timeline, or disappear entirely. This layer can easily exaggerate Nepal's practical build pipeline if read without status discipline.

Explorer label: India basins Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 4
Explorer label: India basins Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 4 Comparison basins outside Nepal-origin control, included to keep Nepal-origin leverage in proportion.

This layer adds non-Nepal comparison basins in northern India. It is a guardrail against making the map too Nepal-centric. The Ganges system has many contributors, and not every Himalayan or plains tributary is controlled by Nepal-origin headwaters.

Use this layer when explaining scale. Compare Nepal-linked basins to India-origin basins to see where Nepal is central and where it is only one part of a much larger downstream system. It is especially useful beside downstream systems and origin/control callou
These are comparison geometries, not the main evidence base for Nepal energy planning. They should not be interpreted as a full inventory of Indian hydrology or water-management infrastructure.

Explorer label: India comparison rivers Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 11

Explorer label: India comparison rivers Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 11 Reference rivers outside Nepal-origin control, included so the map does not overstate Nepal's share of northern India hydrology.

This reference layer shows Indian rivers that help calibrate the Nepal-origin network. It adds visible context for nearby systems that are hydrologically important but not controlled by Nepal-origin basins.
Use it when telling the difference between a Nepal-linked downstream path and the wider northern Indian river network. It is also useful for explaining why downstream influence is real but bounded.
The layer is intentionally selective. It is not a complete Indian river map, and it should not be used as the authoritative source for Indian basin planning.

Explorer label: River name labels Layer group: Text label overlays Feature count: text overlay Permanent river and basin text labels. Turn these on when geography matters more than raw features.

This overlay adds permanent text labels for rivers and basins. It is a readability layer: it helps the user keep the geography in view while inspecting features that may otherwise be visually abstract.
Use it during presentations, screenshots, or when comparing basins and rivers without constantly opening popups. It is especially useful when the map is zoomed to regional scale.
Labels can clutter dense views and may overlap at some zoom levels. Turn them off when project markers or linework need to be inspected closely.

Explorer label: Basin areas Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 4
Explorer label: Basin areas Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 4 Koshi, Gandaki, Karnali, and Mahakali upstream basin polygons, with Nepal share and monsoon context.

This layer frames Nepal as an upstream hydrological actor. The polygons show the major Himalayan systems whose water, sediment, and seasonal flows pass through or originate in Nepal before entering the wider Ganges plain. The layer is not just a geography over

Read basin area together with Nepal share and monsoon share. A large basin with high Nepal share indicates stronger upstream relevance for Nepal planning; a shared or border basin requires more geopolitical caution. The layer is especially useful before turnin

Basin polygons simplify complex hydrology. They do not resolve treaty rights, engineering control, reservoir operations, environmental flows, or sub-basin heterogeneity. The polygons should be read as analytical boundaries, not as claims of unilateral control.
koshi-basin gandaki-basin karnali-basin mahakali-basin ganges-contribution hydro-geopolitics data-map-layer-labels data-map-inventory
Explorer label: Downstream rivers Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 4

Explorer label: Downstream rivers Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 4 Main Nepal-origin systems traced downstream toward the Ganges plain to show where upstream control becomes regional influence.

This layer follows the major Nepal-linked river systems beyond the national boundary. It makes explicit that hydropower planning, sediment, flood timing, and dry-season flow questions do not stop at Nepal border lines.

Use it with basin polygons and downstream impact markers. The layer is strongest when the question is geopolitical or system-scale: where Nepal-origin water goes, which downstream names correspond to Nepal basins, and why India-facing transmission and water re

The traced systems are schematic enough for strategic interpretation, not hydrodynamic modeling. They do not encode barrages, diversions, irrigation withdrawals, or local channel complexity in the plains.

Explorer label: Ocean route Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 4 HydroRIVERS downstream traces continuing Nepal-linked systems through the Ganges/Padma/Meghna route to the Bay of Bengal.

This layer extends the existing data-layer-nepal-origin-downstream-systems beyond the northern plains. It lets the reader follow each Nepal-linked system through the larger Ganges network toward the Bay of Bengal. The four route features are: Route | Origin sy

Use this as a strategic continuity layer. The existing downstream-system lines show Nepal-to-plains routing; this layer shows how those systems enter the shared lower Ganges route and ultimately reach the Bay of Bengal. Important fields: Field | How To Use It

This is a topology and communication layer, not a hydrodynamic model. It does not represent reservoir operations, flow volumes after withdrawals, distributary splits, tidal dynamics, embankments, barrages, irrigation diversions, or floodplain storage. Dense de

Explorer label: Nepal rivers Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 22 HydroRIVERS-derived Nepal tributaries relevant to the power and basin story, including WECS potential where available.

This layer turns the basin story into named river corridors. It uses HydroRIVERS-derived linework curated around hydropower-relevant Nepal tributaries, so the map can connect projects, basins, and downstream systems to specific rivers rather than only broad po

Use it to orient project clusters by river, to see which tributaries feed each basin, and to connect wiki pages about projects to the hydrological system they depend on. It is most useful with hydropower or basin-seasonality layers turned on.

HydroRIVERS linework is good for network context, but it is not a survey-grade river centerline. Some smaller or locally important streams may be absent, simplified, or named differently across sources.

Explorer label: River influence Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 8
Explorer label: River influence Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 8 Interpretive callouts for origin, control class, Nepal share, and downstream identity across the basin stack.

This layer provides short interpretive labels for the hydrology map. It explains whether a system is Nepal-linked, shared, or outside Nepal-origin control, and it ties that classification to downstream names and basin shares.

Use callouts when presenting the map to someone who does not already know the river systems. They are designed to make the control logic visible: where Nepal is upstream, where it is only partly upstream, and where it is not upstream at all.
Control class is analytical shorthand. It should not be read as legal authority, treaty entitlement, or operational control over downstream water infrastructure.

Explorer label: Satellite Layer group: Basemaps Feature count: basemap
Explorer label: Satellite Layer group: Basemaps Feature count: basemap Imagery basemap for visual checking of settlements, reservoirs, roads, river valleys, and corridor anchors.

The satellite basemap provides imagery behind the explorer overlays. It is useful when a map feature needs to be checked against visible land cover, settlements, river channels, reservoirs, road access, or grid-hub surroundings.

Use it for visual sanity checks: whether a point appears to sit on a plausible river reach, whether a corridor anchor is near a settlement or substation area, or whether a floating-PV candidate corresponds to a visible water body. It is the best basemap for vi

Imagery date, cloud cover, georeferencing, and resolution vary. A visible object is not the same thing as verified infrastructure, and an invisible object is not proof that a planned or underground feature does not exist.

Explorer label: Solar resource zones Layer group: Solar layers Feature count: 6
Explorer label: Solar resource zones Layer group: Solar layers Feature count: 6 Coarse solar resource zones. These are strategic zones, not parcel-level siting decisions.

This layer maps broad solar resource geography using GHI interpretation. It places solar in Nepal's spatial energy story, especially the Terai and high-insolation zones that can complement hydro seasonality.

Use it with solar plants, solar suitability, and hydropower seasonality. The question is not simply where sun exists, but where solar can be built quickly enough to help winter deficits and reduce over-dependence on hydro timing.
GHI zones are not project-ready siting. Land access, grid connection, slope, social constraints, and procurement design matter as much as resource quality.

chunks.h: Layer: Solar Plants + NEA 960 MW Awards
chunks.s: Explorer label: Solar plants Layer group: Solar layers Feature count: 88 Operating solar plants plus NEA 960 MW award anchors, with approximate-location flags where needed.
This layer combines known solar plants with NEA 960 MW tender award anchors. It makes the solar pipeline visible as actual places, not only procurement totals.
Use it to see whether solar is spatially concentrated, how close it sits to demand or grid infrastructure, and how utility-scale solar relates to the Terai and existing hydro system.
Some locations are exact DoED coordinates, while others are district, substation, or place anchors. Always read precision fields before treating a point as a site boundary.

Explorer label: Best solar locations Layer group: Solar layers Feature count: 6
Explorer label: Best solar locations Layer group: Solar layers Feature count: 6 Strategic suitability bands combining resource value with constraint notes.
This layer moves beyond sunshine alone. It frames broad areas by strategic suitability, combining resource potential with constraints that affect whether solar can be useful in Nepal's power system.

Use it when comparing solar build logic against hydropower and transmission geography. It is particularly useful for understanding why quick-cycle solar is a different kind of planning lever than large hydro.
Suitability bands are strategic, not parcel-screening output. They should not replace land, grid, environmental, or procurement studies.

Explorer label: Topographic Layer group: Basemaps Feature count: basemap
Explorer label: Topographic Layer group: Basemaps Feature count: basemap Terrain-oriented basemap useful for checking river corridors, valleys, ridge crossings, and Himalayan project context.

The topographic basemap adds relief, contours, and landform cues to the explorer. It helps translate dots and lines into actual Himalayan terrain, especially where hydropower projects, river valleys, and transmission alignments depend on elevation and corridor

Use it when the map question is physical: why a project sits in a particular valley, whether a line appears to cross steep country, how a tributary relates to a basin, or where storage candidates sit relative to mountain terrain. It makes more sense than Posit

The topographic layer is a background reference, not an engineering survey. It can make overlay colors harder to read, and it should not be treated as evidence for exact alignment, gradient, or construction feasibility.

Explorer label: Main network Layer group: Transmission and trade layers Feature count: 22 Public-facing source-controlled transmission corridors, with status and provenance visible in popups.

This is the main transmission layer readers should use. It combines source traces and manual document-grounded traces into a coherent corridor network without hiding geometry role, source, status, or confidence.

Use it to read Nepal's major domestic grid corridors: which are operational, under construction, partially operational, or lower-confidence, and how they relate to hydropower clusters and cross-border gateways. The western 132 kV backbone is now represented he
This is still corridor geometry, not tower-by-tower engineering alignment. Read geometry role, trace confidence, and source fields in the popup when precision matters.

Use this layer for quick orientation or when a corridor does not yet have route-grade public geometry. In the power-system preset, the major transmission network is the preferred default. If this layer shows a connection that the major network does not, that u

This overlay labels the major transmission network directly on the map. It is separated from river labels because the grid story needs its own visual hierarchy.
Use it when the map is being used to explain grid bottlenecks rather than hydrology. Labels are sourced from the major transmission network only, not from QA fragments or the conceptual spine.
Transmission labels are only as good as the underlying corridor abstraction. They help orientation, but they do not add new geometry or confidence.

Explorer label: Not shown as a map-control layer Layer group: Internal transmission audit data Feature count: 56 Generated endpoint and junction nodes used to validate corridor topology.

This point dataset is generated from traced corridor endpoints, inferred junctions, and supporting grid anchors. It helps validate whether the mapped network connects coherently, but it is no longer exposed as a public map control because many nodes are topolo
Use it when maintaining the transmission pipeline: checking whether a line has plausible endpoints, where generated joins are happening, or whether topology is being over-interpreted.
These are internal topology nodes. Some represent real substations or hubs, while others are generated endpoint clusters. Do not read every marker as a surveyed asset.

Explorer label: Not shown as a map-control layer Layer group: Internal transmission audit data Feature count: 22 Source/manual trace fragments preserved behind the scenes for audit and reproducibility.

This dataset preserves the traced segments recovered from transmission maps and reports before the public network presentation is simplified. It is useful for audit trails and geometry QA, but it is no longer exposed as a separate reader-facing map layer becau
Use it when maintaining the pipeline and checking where a corridor trace came from, how many pieces it has, or how a source map was translated into explorer geometry.

Explorer label: Not shown as a map-control layer Layer group: Internal transmission audit data Feature count: 0 Evidence gaps in traced geometry when they exist. These are audit markers, not physical lines.

This dataset is the pipeline's honesty layer for transmission tracing. It records where linework has gaps, breaks, or uncertainty so the public map does not make incomplete geometry look more authoritative than it is. It is currently empty after the latest cor

Use it when validating the grid layers or deciding where source recovery should improve next. It explains why a corridor may appear disconnected or why a connected-network layer includes inferred segments.
An audit gap is not necessarily a real-world gap in the grid. It is a gap in the reconstructed map evidence or tracing confidence.

chunks.s: Factor | Norway | Nepal | --------|--------|-------| Storage | 1,401 MW pumped + extensive reservoir capacity; ~87 TWh reservoir storage | Zero pumped storage; 85 MCM active reservoir (Kulekhani only) | Grid | Mature 420/300/132 kV grid; fully interconnected w

chunks.h: The Bhutan Model: Why It's Not Copyable
chunks.s: bhutan-hydropower-model works because: 1. Population: 0.8M vs Nepal's 30M — surplus per capita is massive 2. Single buyer: India absorbs virtually all Bhutanese exports under guaranteed bilateral PPAs at fixed rates 3. Fiscal structure: Hydro royalties + divid

Laos has expanded aggressively (9,760 MW installed) through Chinese and Thai investment, targeting 13 GW by 2030. But: Debt burden: Multiple dam projects are debt-financed by Chinese state banks; repayment dependent on Thai electricity purchases Environmental

A 2021 Oxford study identified ~2,800 off-river pumped hydro sites in Nepal with combined storage capacity of ~50 TWh. Nepal needs only ~1.5 TWh of storage to balance a 100% renewable system at advanced-economy consumption levels. This means Nepal has 17x more

Grouped bar chart: renewable W/capita for Norway, Bhutan, Laos, Nepal, World Average. Nepal's bar should be barely visible at Norway's scale — that visual asymmetry is the argument. Second chart: potential-to-actual ratio as a scatter plot, with Nepal as the e

A two-phase reproducible model quantifying Nepal's winter electricity gap at both monthly energy (GWh) and sub-daily capacity (MW) resolution. Phase 1 ( ) models Dec–Feb monthly energy balances under parameterised 2030/2035 scenarios. Phase 2 ( ) models the evening peak hour (18:

A two-phase reproducible model quantifying Nepal's winter electricity gap at both monthly energy (GWh) and sub-daily capacity (MW) resolution. Phase 1 (scripts/buildwinterdeficitmodel.py) models Dec–Feb monthly energy balances under parameterised 2030/2035 sce

Phase 1 reads NEA FY 2024/25 monthly energy data as baseline, applies scenario assumptions (solar deployment, storage hydro additions, demand growth, BESS, demand-shaping), and outputs monthly Dec–Feb balances. Dec–Feb = Poush + Magh (two BS months, 58 days).

Phase 2 takes Magh (mid-Jan to mid-Feb, ≈February) as the canonical winter reference month and models the 18:30 evening peak dispatch. The diurnal profile is sourced from data-solar-hydro-complementarity-profile lines 71–85: February weekday demand shape norma

35withbudhigandaki | 4,066 | 1,504 | 1,809 | 0 | 500 | 400 | −147 | −3.6% | Model parameters: Magh reference month, 29 days, profile-implied peak-to-average ratio 1.348, reservoir dispatch factor 0.9, solarmwatpeak always 0.0 at the default 18:30 target hour.

Scenario names are inherited from Phase 1 energy-balance design. Note that 2035solaronly excludes risky storage (Dudhkoshi) and 2035nosolar includes it — the labels describe the additional resource being tested, not what is excluded. Scenario ID | Solar MWp |

Nepal's energy problem is not that it lacks electricity — it's that it produces enormous monsoon surplus (700–800 MW of daily spill during wet season, stranded-generation) while importing expensive winter power and burning imported LPG for cooking. The energy-

on (seasonal-arbitrage-trap). Creates baseload for the hydro fleet. Cooking load is predictable, schedulable, and partially shiftable. Evening cooking (18:30–19:25) coincides with the daily peak, which is a problem. But midday cooking (a significant share in N

VERIFIED: Electric cooking penetration: ~0.5% of households (2021 WB estimate, up from 0.1% in 2011). 92.2% of households use electricity for lighting, but cooking remains dominated by biomass and imported LPG. See data-domestic-demand. Residential electricity

RIFIED: No Nepal-specific cooking load timing profile study exists in the wiki. Understanding the temporal distribution of Nepali cooking energy demand (breakfast, midday, evening shares; seasonal variation; urban vs. rural patterns) is critical to sizing the

Domestic demand at export-relevant scale. Shifting 20–30% of cooking to electricity creates ~50–75 PJ of new demand — roughly doubling current electricity consumption. For comparison, Nepal's total electricity export was 2,380 GWh (~8.6 PJ) in FY 2024/25. Cook

ation from fuelwood collection is reduced. Women's time and labour. Fuelwood collection and biomass cooking are overwhelmingly performed by women. Electrification directly reduces this unpaid labour burden.

The NEA Triple-Authority Conflict (nea-triple-authority) is not an edge-case governance flaw — it is the institutional mechanism that converts Nepal's hydrological advantage into stranded assets and retail investor losses. When NLDC curtails a project for "gri

PARTIALLY VERIFIED: The 2003 World Bank Nepal power sector review recommended eliminating conflicts of interest in NEA's structure. No legislation has been enacted in the 23 years since. A draft Open Access policy exists and has been discussed since at least 2

End of uncompensated curtailment. An independent ISO would make dispatch decisions on technical merit, not commercial self-interest. NLDC curtailment decisions affecting billions of rupees in annual IPP revenue would be subject to published criteria and regula

ling of structural separation's NPV for Nepal's power sector exists in the wiki. The 37.1% revenue advantage Chilime enjoys over comparable private IPPs through its subsidiary PPA architecture (chilime) provides a directional benchmark: a 20-25% reduction in t

PPA clause text. The most important unresolved legal question: does any standard PPA contain an exemption for curtailment-ordered shortfalls? Primary PPA text has not been accessed. Resolving this would confirm or refute the double-bind's legal basis. NLDC dis

cess regulation (simplest, most precedent internationally) → ISO legislation → penalty transfer The window of a majority government (current post-2024) is the most favourable in a decade for structural reform. The risk is that the political cycle turns before

al lender requirements — all three major MDBs (World Bank, ADB, IFC) already mandate climate-adjusted hydrology as a non-negotiable condition for project financing. DoED's failure to mandate equivalent standards creates a two-tier system where mega-projects (U
